messages.content:
Hi guys, I’m experimenting with building a file caching layer inspired by the “needle in a haystack” approach used by Facebook Photos Storage and by SeaweedFS. The idea is to store many small files (chunks) inside a single large file, and then use byte offsets + lengths for retrieval.

What I’ve done so far:

Implemented a benchmark comparing two methods:

The “normal” approach → opening and reading separate small files.

My approach : seeking into a single large file at specific byte ranges.

Ran benchmarks locally, but the results showed no significant performance difference between the two methods.

The problem I’m facing:

I was expecting the “chunked large file” approach to clearly outperform the many-small-files approach.

From what I’ve read, this method is proven in real-world systems like Facebook Photos and SeaweedFS, so I know there must be a context where it shines.

I suspect my benchmark is not simulating the real pain points (e.g. metadata lookups, file descriptor limits, network/distributed FS latency, concurrency).

My question: 
How should I design my benchmarks so they reflect the actual advantages of this approach?

well, you will always be promted with some stuff, which it want access to like discord bots.
so you will know it.
I don't understand your statement
It does not access any bots or anything
Just username and access the names of server I'm in
To check if you are in that server
And if you have the patreon role on the server

sry my bad.
I mean when you will login into any stuff like that from discord shit it will always tell you what stuff its actually accessing (like when adding discord bots)
well if thats the only thing its accessing then there is no problem.
You have no privacy in discord just to clarify things
I'm aware
You can't even completely delete your account it will just be unavailable to you
I just wanted to know if the thing I watch on that website are somehow being tracked/linked to discord
Which might not be the case
Yes ofc they know who you are in discord
It's like the midjourney shit
No I mean like, can they track the categories of goon material I watch on that website
Have you ever tried it before?
Ofc bro
Nope
They cannot
Website always save the requests they get for security reasons
Mera kehna tha
So technically if they want to track you they can
That's how they spot ddos attacks like how did you thought they do it
Ki can they like link those requests to the discord acc
Like OZeus watched 6hr MILF content. OZeus being my dc name
Idc if those requests stay in their servers and whatnot
I see
You need to understand how accounts work let me explain it to you first
Yes please

You give them an email or DC account or something,they give you an id linked to that email or discord account that differentiates you from other users (e.g: 27241),from that account you get a hash in the browser's cookies to keep you connected without needing to login again.


When talking about watching vids on that site ,they can see who is watching because you are fetching data from that server with your id,and also they can know how much time you watched stuff based on those requests (cuz when you are watching stuff your browser is always receiving data of the videos because it's sent in segments usually using the .dash format , it's like sending each time an https request).
And finally yes they can track who is watching what cuz it's just a matter of searching the requests and searching for your id and linking it to your account
I hope you understood
I tried to simplify it as much as possible
Thanks

Cool/Unique Backend project in resume that sets you apart.:happyblush: 


im curious to hear about cool or unique backend projects you've worked on that you feel really differentiate you as a developer.
could be things like:

1. a system you designed from scratch 

2. an interesting architectural decision 

3. a tough scaling or performance problem 

4. a weird but effective tech stack 

5. a real-world problem you solved creatively what did you build, what made it challenging, and what did you learn from it? 

thanks looking forward to reading your stories!

Hey, I’m actually building something that fits point 5. It’s my first real project I’m putting out publicly.
Made OneTimeShare – a simple privacy-focused one-time text/file sharing app with expiring links and minimal data retention.
Built everything from scratch, handled token flow, expiry logic, cleanup, and basic abuse control myself. Learned a lot about backend design and security tradeoffs while doing it.
Repo: https://github.com/Aayushbankar/onetimeshare
Any honest reviews are welcome.
